findings.title: SQL parameterization verified — all queries use ? placeholders
findings.description: LearningStore (storage/learning_store.py) uses parameterized queries exclusively. All db.execute() calls use ? placeholders with tuple parameters. Verified across all methods: get_entity_memory(entity_id) line 269-271, update_entity_memory(entity_id, data) lines 289-309, plus predictions, lessons, and calibration methods. The class docstring explicitly states 'All methods use parameterized queries.' No string concatenation or f-string interpolation in SQL.

findings.title: entity_id sources are controlled — keyword matching and ticker symbols
findings.description: identify_entities() produces entity_ids from three sources: 1) _CB_KEYWORDS hardcoded map (ecb→ECB, fed→Fed, etc.), 2) _SECTOR_KEYWORDS hardcoded map, 3) _INDICATOR_KEYWORDS hardcoded map, 4) ticker symbols from asset_class_routing. None of these sources involve unsanitized user input flowing directly into entity_id. Even if malicious text matched a keyword, the resulting entity_id is from the hardcoded value map, not the input text.

findings.title: JSON deserialization uses json.loads — safe
findings.description: All deserialization from stored JSON strings uses json.loads() which does not execute code. No eval(), exec(), or pickle usage. The stored JSON comes from internally-generated data (StructuredLesson fields, root_cause taxonomy keys), not raw user input.

findings.title: recent_lessons stores lesson dicts including LLM-generated updated_belief
findings.description: update_entity_memory() stores the full lesson dict (including 'updated_belief' from LLM output) in recent_lessons (line 152). This LLM-generated text is stored in SQLite via parameterized query (safe from SQL injection) and later retrieved via json.loads (safe from code execution). The data is surfaced through generate_methodology_injection() in expertise_discovery, which applies validate_distillation_safety() checks. Direct prompt injection from stored data is mitigated by: 1) parameterized storage, 2) json.loads deserialization, 3) downstream safety validation. The residual risk is that a compromised LLM response could inject text that passes safety checks — but this requires a compromised LLM, which is the threat model's boundary.
findings.remediation: LOW priority. Consider storing a truncated/sanitized version of updated_belief in recent_lessons (e.g., first 200 chars) to limit blast radius.

findings.title: common_blind_spots limited to 10 entries
findings.description: Line 162: if len(mem.common_blind_spots) > 10, trim to last 10. Prevents unbounded growth from repeated lessons.

notes: SQL parameterization verified across all LearningStore methods. Entity IDs come from controlled keyword maps, not raw user input. JSON serialization/deserialization is safe. The residual risk around stored LLM-generated text is inherent to the learning architecture and is mitigated by downstream safety validation in expertise_discovery.
